title: Huntress for the engineer running the deployment, final check

questions.prompt: Which of these are partner-configured rather than Huntress-managed? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Managed Defender policies (Audit / Enforce, settings)
Managed Defender exclusions (paths, processes, extensions)
Process Insights detection logic
ITDR detection logic
Ransomware Canary file rollout (on/off and scope)
questions.explanation: Defender policies, Defender exclusions, and Ransomware Canary rollout are partner-configurable. Process Insights and ITDR detection logic are managed by the Huntress SOC.

questions.prompt: Three customers in the same vertical all need the same Defender exclusion for a vendor application. What's the right scope decision?
choices: Add the exclusion at the Endpoint level on each affected machine
Add the exclusion at each customer's Organization level
Promote the exclusion to Account level
Disable Managed AV for that application's process across all customers
questions.explanation: Three customers needing the same exclusion is a cross-customer signal; promote to Account level. Per-Organization or per-Endpoint copies create maintenance debt.

questions.prompt: An endpoint with Defender disabled and a third-party AV active will show as Not Protected (red) in Managed AV.
questions.explanation: Managed AV reports Defender's status. Defender disabled = Not Protected from Defender's perspective. The endpoint may be protected by the third-party AV; the dashboard is reporting Defender, not aggregate protection.

questions.prompt: Where do you set Tamper Protection on Microsoft Defender for a customer's estate?
choices: In the Huntress Managed AV policy settings
Via Intune, GPO/M365 Defender portal, or locally on each endpoint, depending on the customer's licensing
Through the Huntress Process Insights configuration
By contacting Huntress Product Support to enable it at the account level
questions.explanation: Tamper Protection is reported on by Huntress but managed via Microsoft's own controls, Intune for Intune-managed devices, M365 Defender portal where licensing supports it, or local for one-off.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are direct M365 ITDR integration prerequisites? (Select all that apply.)
choices: An Account-level Admin user in Huntress
A Microsoft 365 user with Global Administrator
At least one active Exchange Online license in the tenant
Audit logs enabled in M365
An on-premises Active Directory Domain Controller
questions.explanation: Admin Huntress, Global Admin M365, Exchange Online license, audit logs on. AD DC is not part of the ITDR onboarding requirements.

questions.prompt: Huntress reports MFA as Enabled for any user with any third-party MFA product configured.
questions.explanation: Per the ITDR FAQ, the Huntress portal only reports MFA as Enabled when the user uses Microsoft MFA via the Microsoft Authenticator app. Third-party MFA is not reflected as Enabled in the portal.

questions.prompt: After successfully integrating an M365 tenant, how long should you wait before treating 'no data flowing' as a problem?
choices: Up to 1 hour
Up to 4 hours
Up to 24 hours (longer for legacy tenants)
Up to 7 days
questions.explanation: The documentation states data may take up to 24 hours, longer for legacy tenants. Past 24 hours, check audit logging and the Organization Management role group before opening a support case.

questions.prompt: What's the right purpose of an SAT customer's first phishing campaign?
choices: A challenging vertical-specific scenario to find the worst offenders
A generic scenario to establish a baseline before any tailored campaigns
Skip phishing entirely; start with assignments
A test campaign sent to admins only to confirm allowlisting
questions.explanation: First campaign is for the baseline. A generic scenario gives a clean reading. Vertical-tailored campaigns come after the baseline is established and after assignments have been delivered.

questions.prompt: If the customer uses both M365 and a third-party email security gateway, the Huntress SAT phishing IPs and domains need to be allowlisted in both.
questions.explanation: Per the Manage Phishing Campaigns guidance, additional email security tools require their own allowlisting. Allowlisting only one of the two layers leaves the other to filter the simulation.

questions.prompt: Huntress supports multiple PSA integrations active at the same time, allowing dual-PSA setups.
questions.explanation: Only one PSA integration is supported at a time. The Email integration runs alongside as a secondary destination.

questions.prompt: PSA Auto-Map matches Huntress Organizations to PSA companies on what basis?
choices: Fuzzy match (e.g. 'Able Moose' to 'Able Moose Accounting')
Exact name match
First three characters of the name
PSA company ID stored as a Huntress Organization tag
questions.explanation: Auto-Map works on exact name matches. Misalignment requires either fixing one side's name or manually mapping. Fuzzy matching isn't documented behaviour.

questions.prompt: After an Autotask PSA integration is configured with the *Set Status after Huntress Remediation* field, the PSA ticket status updates automatically when remediation is approved or rejected on the Huntress side.
questions.explanation: The Autotask integration documents this exact behaviour: the configured status maps to the Huntress remediation outcome, closing the loop between platforms.
